coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent responsive film based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots embedded in mesoporous silica for visual detection of Cr(VI)
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Jiabao
Su, Miao
Yuan, Haosen
Jing, Cai
Li, Bihao
Wang, Binsong
coredata.dc:description: Traditional dispersive active sensing materials must be discarded as water-soluble waste after use, potentially causing secondary pollution. In this study, a solid-state sensing platform was constructed by embedding in situ synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) into mesoporous molecular sieves and combining them with a polyethylene glycol-alginate composite membrane, resulting in the NCQDs@SBA15/PVA/SA (NCSPS) fluorescence-responsive film. NCSPS demonstrated excellent specificity for detecting hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)), enabling detection through a significant and intuitive color change, with a detection limit of 62.4 nM. NCSPS exhibited outstanding stability, selectivity, and strong anti-interference ability. In tests using Songhua River water samples, the spiked recovery rate of Cr(VI) ranged from 93.41 % to 97.06 %, further verifying its practical application potential. Through UV-Vis and fluorescence lifetime analysis, this study revealed that the fluorescence detection mechanism originates from electrostatic interactions (EI) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Additionally, to advance portable on-site detection of Cr(VI), NCSPS was successfully integrated with a smartphone device, developing a portable smartphone fluorescence sensing platform with a detection limit of 76 nM. This platform significantly simplifies the detection process, reducing time and cost, and provides new directions and technological breakthroughs for the research and development of portable solid-state fluorescence detection devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solid-state sensing platforms
NCQDs
SBA15
Fluorescent responsive film
Smartphone fluorescence sensing platform

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The detection experimental of NCSPS for Cr(VI)
Optimization and ideal conditions of NCSPS
Fluorescence response of NCSPS to Cr (VI)
Fluorescence Selectivity of NCSPS for Cr(VI)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the NSFC project (21971057), with funding provided by the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (YQ2019B006) and Foundation of Heilongjiang Educational Committee (12531009).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme for the preparation of the NCSPS by introducing NCQDs@SBA15 into the composite film.

(a) SEM images of SBA15; (b) SEM images of the surface of NCSPS; (c) Cross-section images of the NCSPS (inset: The enlarged cross-sectional image embedded with SBA15); (d-e) TEM image and (f) HRTEM image of NCQDs; (g) TEM image of SBA-15; (h-i) HRTEM image of NCQDs@SBA15.
(a-d) XPS spectrum of C1s, N1s, O1s and Si2p for NCSPS; (e) FTIR spectra of PVA/SA film and NCSPS; (f) XRD patterns of NCQDs, NCQDs@SBA15, PVA/SA film, and NCSPS.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of NCQDs@SBA15 at different concentrations under optimal excitation at 349 nm (Inset: Correspondence between NCQDs@SBA15 concentrations and F
0
/F fluorescence intensity). (b) Fluorescence spectra of NCSPS showing the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths.

(a) Fluorescence response of NCQDs@SBA15 and (c) NCSPS in the presence of increasing concentrations of Cr(VI); (b) Relative fluorescence intensites (F
0/
F) of NCQDs@SBA15 and (d) NCSPS added to different concentrations of Cr(VI) (inset: The linear correlation between F
0
/F and the Cr(VI) concentration).

(a) The selectivity of the PL response of NCSPS after adding metal ions at a concentration of 500 μM (b) The anti-interference capability of NCSPS; (c) Fluorescence emission intensity changes of NCQDs@SBA15 and (d) NCSPS observed continuously over a period of 7 weeks; The photographs of (e) NCSPS and (f) PVA/SA film under 349 nm UV light; (g) UV-Visible absorption spectra of NCSPS and NCSPS+Cr(Ⅵ); (h) Comparison of the emission spectra of Cr(VI) with the excitation and emission spectra of NCSPS; (i) PL lifetime data of NCSPS and NCSPS+ Cr(Ⅵ).

(a) Sensing mechanism of Cr(Ⅵ) by NCSPS; (b) Portable concentration detection system compatible with smartphones; (c) Cr(Ⅵ) Quick Test APP; (d) Associated RGB values across varying concentrations of Cr(VI) (inset: Range from 0 to 100 µM); Reproducibility of NCSPS response to Cr(VI) at 3 µM.
Detection of Cr(Ⅵ) by using NCSPS in water samples.
The detection Limit Summary in this work.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent responsive film based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots embedded in mesoporous silica for visual detection of Cr(VI)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Heilongjiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Environmental Nanotechnology, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Heilongjiang University, Harbin 150080, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Heilongjiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Environmental Nanotechnology, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Heilongjiang University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Heilongjiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Environmental Nanotechnology, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Heilongjiang University, Harbin 150080, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Traditional dispersive active sensing materials must be discarded as water-soluble waste after use, potentially causing secondary pollution. In this study, a solid-state sensing platform was constructed by embedding in situ synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) into mesoporous molecular sieves and combining them with a polyethylene glycol-alginate composite membrane, resulting in the NCQDs@SBA15/PVA/SA (NCSPS) fluorescence-responsive film. NCSPS demonstrated excellent specificity for detecting hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)), enabling detection through a significant and intuitive color change, with a detection limit of 62.4 nM. NCSPS exhibited outstanding stability, selectivity, and strong anti-interference ability. In tests using Songhua River water samples, the spiked recovery rate of Cr(VI) ranged from 93.41 % to 97.06 %, further verifying its practical application potential. Through UV-Vis and fluorescence lifetime analysis, this study revealed that the fluorescence detection mechanism originates from electrostatic interactions (EI) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Additionally, to advance portable on-site detection of Cr(VI), NCSPS was successfully integrated with a smartphone device, developing a portable smartphone fluorescence sensing platform with a detection limit of 76 nM. This platform significantly simplifies the detection process, reducing time and cost, and provides new directions and technological breakthroughs for the research and development of portable solid-state fluorescence detection devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium is a hazardous heavy metal widely used in industries such as metal electroplating, ink manufacturing, leather tanning, and textile production [1]. Its hexavalent form, Cr(VI), exhibits carcinogenic, teratogenic, and mutagenic effects on animals and humans and poses significant threats to ecosystems and the environment due to its high mobility [2]. Therefore, trace analysis of Cr(VI) in water samples is of great importance. Common methods for Cr(VI) detection include voltammetry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), UV–visible absorption spectroscopy (UV–vis), atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), and X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy (XRF) [3]. However, these techniques are relatively complex and costly, making them unsuitable for widespread application [4]. As a result, there is an urgent need for simpler alternative methods. Fluorescence detection technology has attracted considerable attention due to its simplicity, high efficiency, and low cost. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), with their remarkable fluorescence properties and ability to emit strong light at specific wavelengths (400–700 nm) [4], are regarded as ideal materials for fluorescence detection.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are quasi-spherical nanoparticle-based fluorescent probes with notable advantages such as simple preparation, low cost, high photochemical stability, and excellent fluorescence properties [5]. They have been widely applied in metal ion detection, sensing, drug delivery, and bioimaging [6]. For example, Zhu et al. synthesized nitrogen and boron co-doped CQDs (N,B-CQDs) from lignin and successfully applied them for Cr(VI) detection in water. This material exhibited triple fluorescence with purple, blue, and green emissions at different wavelengths, achieving detection limits of 0.054, 0.049, and 0.077 μM, respectively, demonstrating high sensitivity [7]. However, due to the nanoscale nature of CQDs, particle aggregation can occur, leading to fluorescence quenching and a reduction in detection stability and sensitivity [8].

To address the aggregation of CQDs in solid-state environments, which causes surface defects and fluorescence quenching, our research group proposed using mesoporous silica molecular sieve SBA-15 as a nanoreactor to confine CQD precursors within its pores during synthesis [9]. SBA-15 features abundant pore sizes (5–30 nm) [10], providing a uniform reaction environment and ample space for CQD growth [11,12]. Additionally, its excellent optical transparency avoids interference with the fluorescence properties of CQDs [13]. This method effectively prevents aggregation of bare CQDs under solid-state conditions, significantly improving their stability and practicality.

Traditional fluorescence detection predominantly employs liquid-phase sensing, dispersing CQDs in solution. However, this approach has limitations such as stringent storage conditions, difficulty in recovery, and the risk of secondary pollution after use [14]. In contrast, solid-state sensing platforms, with their compact and convenient characteristics, are more flexible for transportation and deployment in various environments and exhibit greater durability and reliability under harsh conditions [15]. Solid-state materials also offer longer service life and enhanced stability, reducing maintenance costs and minimizing user exposure to hazardous chemicals [16]. Therefore, fabricating CQDs into thin films to construct solid-state sensing platforms is a critical path to overcoming the limitations of liquid-phase detection and offers new avenues for portable sensor development [17].

In film fabrication, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is widely used due to its excellent thermal, mechanical, and chemical stability [18]. Additionally, sodium alginate (SA), a natural polysaccharide extracted from brown algae [19], is often employed to modify films due to its non-toxicity, biodegradability, and excellent film-forming properties [20]. PVA-SA composite films not only possess non-toxic and environmentally friendly characteristics but also exhibit outstanding mechanical strength and thermal stability [21]. By incorporating crosslinking agents, the hydrophilicity of the films can be further enhanced, ensuring rapid access of pollutants to the detection sites while maintaining the films' mechanical strength without compromising detection sensitivity [22]. This design of a solid-state film-based sensing platform provides an innovative solution for convenient, green, and efficient Cr(VI) detection.

Herein, using polyethyleneimine as the nitrogen source and citric acid as the carbon source, this study synthesized the composite material NCQDs@SBA15 based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots in situ within the mesoporous molecular sieve SBA15 via a one-pot hydrothermal method. Subsequently, NCQDs@SBA15 was incorporated into a polyvinyl alcohol-sodium alginate film (PVA/SA film) and subjected to hydrophobic modification using glutaraldehyde vapor, resulting in the successful creation of a composite film material with stable fluorescence, termed NCSPS. NCSPS retains the optical and photochemical properties of NCQDs@SBA15 while exhibiting outstanding specific recognition ability for Cr(VI). Additionally, for on-site rapid detection of Cr(VI), NCSPS was fashioned into small strips and combined with a smartphone to establish Smartphone fluorescence sensing platform. As the concentration of Cr(VI) added to NCSPS increases, the fluorescence signal gradually decreases, and NCSPS transitions from blue to dark yellow, displaying a pronounced color change. By capturing the color changes of NCSPS using a smartphone and analyzing the images with a custom application, the corresponding RGB values are obtained to deduce the concentration of Cr(VI) in water samples, achieving rapid quantitative detection of Cr(VI) concentration. This research extends the utility of fluorescent probes, opening avenues for on-site analysis, and explores novel directions in the advancement of portable solid-state fluorescence detection devices.

Polyethyleneimine (PEI), sodium alginate (SA), citric acid (CA), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), glutaraldehyde (GA), and calcium chloride (CaCl2) were all purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. PEO-PPO-PEO (P123; average Mn∼5800; EO:PO=3:7) was supplied by Tianjin Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. Hydrochloric acid (AR; HCl) from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory, and ethanol from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Deionized water was used throughout the entire experimental process. Other reagents used include MgSO4•7 H2O, NaCl, ZnSO4, Cd(NO3)2•4 H2O, K2Cr2O7, Pb(NO3)2 (Weifang Gongchuang Chemical Co., Ltd.), AlCl3, AgNO3, La(NO3)3•6 H2O (Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd.), KCl (Tianjin Kemeio Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), and MnSO4 (Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd.).
The specific experimental procedure is detailed in Section 1.1 of the Supporting Information.

First, dissolve 0.166 g of citric acid, 1.575 g of PEI [23], and 0.05 g of SBA15 in 50 mL of deionized water. Stir the solution at 400 rpm for 0.5 h at room temperature. Then, transfer the solution to a hydrothermal reaction vessel and heat it at 180°C for 20 h. Afterward, dialyze the solution with deionized water (using a dialysis bag, Viskase, MW3500) for 3 days to remove any unreacted reagents. Store the solution in the dark.

Heat 46.5 mL of distilled water in a beaker on a magnetic stirrer until it reaches ∼80°C. Gradually add 3 g of polyvinyl alcohol and 0.5 g of sodium alginate while stirring at 200 rpm, keeping the temperature at 90°C. Stir for 3 hours until fully dissolved. After cooling to room temperature, add various volumes of NCQDs@SBA15 diluted at a 1:10 ratio. Using a coating device, quickly spread the solution on a plastic substrate to form a film (wet film thickness: 1000 μm). Dry the coated substrate in a 40°C vacuum oven for 10 hours to prepare NCSPS. Similarly, synthesize a control PVA/SA film without NCQDs@SBA15.

Crosslink NCSPS with glutaraldehyde vapor in a sealed glass container to reduce hydrophilicity. Place 50 mL of 25 % glutaraldehyde solution in the container, position NCSPS on a porous support in the reaction box, and react at 50°C for 4 hours. After crosslinking, ventilate NCSPS in a fume hood for 12 hours to remove residual glutaraldehyde vapor.
Details of the characterization are provided in Section 1.2 of the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection experimental of NCSPS for Cr(VI)

ce:section.ce:para: During the detection experiments, the NCSPS was cut into strips with a length of 3 cm and a width of 1 cm for ease of use. Standard curve experiment for Cr(VI): Different concentrations of Cr(VI) solutions (0–3000 μM/L) were added (1 mL) to the prepared NCSPS and left for 3 minutes. The fluorescence intensity was then measured, and photoluminescence spectra were plotted using the blank sample as the baseline. Selectivity experiment for Cr(VI): A series of metal ion stock solutions at a concentration of 500 μM/L (Pb2 +, Mg2+, K+, Na+, Mn2+, Ag+, Al3+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, La3+) were prepared, and fluorescence intensity was recorded using the same method as the standard curve experiment. Actual water sample detection: Solutions of Cr(VI) at different concentrations were prepared using ultrapure water, tap water, and Songhua River water (Harbin, Heilongjiang, China), and fluorescence intensity was tested, with spiked recovery rates calculated. All experiments were repeated three times, and the average values were taken as the raw data to build the relationship between RGB values and fluorescence intensity.

Based on smartphone-based quantitative analysis of Cr(VI), drip 0.1 mL of Cr(VI) with different concentrations onto the prepared fluorescent responsive film. After 5 minutes of equilibrium, fluorescence images of NCSPS were captured using a smartphone under 349 nm UV excitation. Subsequently, a self-developed application is used to analyze the RGB values from the captured images, allowing the determination of the corresponding Cr(VI) concentration. The calculation formula for RGB values is as follows.
(1) RGB values = Blue /(Red + Green)

The morphology of both SBA15 and NCSPS was examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in
Fig. 2a, individual SBA15 exhibits a rod-like cylindrical structure. Particle size analysis of SBA15 (Fig. S1a) reveals that the standard length of SBA15 is approximately 0.84 μm with a width of about 0.43 μm. Fig. 2b shows the surface morphology of NCSPS, which appears smooth, flat, and dense without any cracks. The PVA phase is tightly cross-linked with the SA phase, with no phase separation observed [24]. From the cross-section of NCSPS (Fig. 2c), it can be observed that the NCQDs@SBA15 particles are firmly and uniformly embedded within NCSPS. Additionally, BET analysis results (Fig.S2) indicate an average pore size of 9.827 nm in NCSPS. For detailed BET analysis, please refer to the supporting information. These abundant pores facilitate rapid contact between Cr(VI) in the sample and NCQDs@SBA15 during detection, ensuring rapid detection of Cr(VI) by NCSPS [25].

Fig. 2(d-f) shows the TEM images of NCQDs, where it is evident that the prepared NCQDs exhibit a spherical structure with uniform size and good dispersion, with lattice spacing of around 0.22 nm. Subsequently, the particle size of NCQDs was analyzed, with results shown in Fig.S1b indicating an average particle size of approximately 5.24 nm. Additionally, Fig. 2g shows the TEM image of SBA15, compared to the TEM image of NCQDs@SBA15 in Fig. 2(h-i)，after loading with carbon dots, the morphology of SBA15 remains unchanged, still exhibiting mesoporous channels and a highly ordered two-dimensional hexagonal p6mm symmetry, featuring a hexagonal lattice arrangement [26]. This facilitates the ordered anchoring of NCQDs on SBA15, reducing fluorescence quenching caused by the aggregation of nanoparticles [27] and providing strong assurance for the effective synthesis of NCQDs@SBA15 composite materials.

The material composition of NCSPS was investigated through XPS analysis. Fig.S3 illustrates the XPS spectra of the PVA/SA film. In Fig.S3c, three peaks can be observed at 286.17, 288.84, and 284.8 eV in the C1s spectrum, corresponding to C-O, CO and CC/C-C bonds, respectively [28]. Additionally, the peak at 532.14 eV in the O1s spectrum represents the CO bond, collectively demonstrating the presence of SA, indicating the successful synthesis of the PVA/SA film.

The XPS spectra of NCSPS are depicted in Fig. 3(a-d). In Fig. 3a, three peaks in the C1s region at 285.97 eV (C-O/C-N), 284.80 eV (CC/C-C), and 288.82 eV (CO) show shifts, indicating structural changes in the PVA/SA film due to the addition of NCQDs@SBA15 [29]. Furthermore, a new peak associated with -CONH- appears at 531.11 eV in the O1s region in Fig. 3c, likely resulting from chemical reactions between carboxyl (-COOH) and amino (-NH2) groups induced by GA [30], indicating the effective binding of NCQDs@SBA15 to the PVA/SA film substrate. As shown in Fig. 3b, the peak at 404.65 eV in the N1s region corresponds to the N-Si bond, which, combined with the Si-O bond at 532.6 eV in the O1s region [31], further confirms the presence of Si elements, demonstrating the successful loading of SBA15 into NCSPS.

FT-IR analysis was further employed to investigate the internal structures of PVA/SA film and NCSPS, as illustrated in Fig. 3e. In PVA/SA film, the absorption peak at 1099 cm−1 corresponds to the asymmetric stretching vibration of the C-O-C groups, while the peaks at 1634 cm−1 and 1424 cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations, respectively, of the -COO− groups in SA [32]. The hydrogen bonding stretching peak in PVA/SA film shifted from 3419 cm−1 to 3411 cm−1 in NCSPS, indicating the formation of hydrogen bonds between polyvinyl alcohol, sodium alginate, and NCQDs [33]. For NCSPS, the vibration peak at 478 cm−1 corresponds to the Si-O-Si bond within the SBA15 framework, while the characteristic absorption band at 853 cm−1 can be linked to the vibration of the Si-O groups [34]. The peaks at 1578 cm−1 and 1379 cm−1 represent the N-H bond and the C-N bond, respectively [35]. The significant increase in peaks within the range of 3100 cm−1 to 3300 cm−1 in NCSPS indicates a substantial increase in -OH and -NH2 groups in the material, confirming successful cross-linking of NCQDs@SBA15 [36]. Finally, the absorption peak at 1745 cm−1 corresponds to the carbonyl groups (CO) formed by the cross-linking of the carboxyl groups in SA and the amino groups in NCQDs@SBA15 by GA, collectively demonstrating the successful conjugation of NCQDs@SBA15 via the Schiff base reaction onto PVA/SA film [37]. Moreover, similar results obtained from multiple sample tests confirm the reproducibility of the material.

The synthesis of the materials was analyzed using XRD patterns of the raw materials, depicted in Fig. 3 f. The broad peak in NCQDs corresponds to the 002 diffraction line in carbon quantum dots [38]. Therefore, the broad band at 2θ values of 15–35° in NCQDs@SBA15 indicates the successful in situ synthesis of NCQDs in SBA15, while also confirming the successful immobilization of NCQDs@SBA15 in NCSPS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization and ideal conditions of NCSPS
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve higher fluorescence intensity and a more sensitive response system for NCSPS, and to improve the utilization efficiency of NCQDs@SBA15, the influence of different NCQDs@SBA15 addition ratios on the fluorescence properties of NCSPS was investigated. A series of NCSPS with different concentrations of NCQDs@SBA15 solution to film solution ratios (0–80 %) were prepared. As shown in the inset of Fig. 4a, the fluorescence intensity of NCSPS initially increased and then decreased with the rise in the concentration ratio of NCQDs@SBA15. When the concentration ratio of NCQDs@SBA15 is below 20 %, the content of NCQDs@SBA15 in the film is too low to exhibit significant fluorescence after uniform dispersion, resulting in low fluorescence intensity. Meanwhile, as the concentration ratio of NCQDs@SBA15 in the film exceeds 20 %, the higher concentration of carbon quantum dots leads to reduced dispersion, causing fluorescence aggregation and self-quenching, ultimately leading to a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of NCSPS. Therefore, considering achieving the best fluorescence performance of NCSPS and economic benefits, an NCQDs@SBA15 concentration ratio of 20 % was chosen for subsequent experiments, and all samples were prepared using this ratio.

The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of NCSPS were further investigated by measuring its fluorescence intensity under excitation wavelengths ranging from 289 to 389 nm (Fig.S4). The results indicate that the optimal excitation wavelength for NCSPS is 349 nm, and its fluorescence emission exhibits no significant excitation dependency [39]. As shown in Fig. 4b, under 349 nm excitation, NCSPS exhibits a maximum fluorescence emission wavelength at 445 nm. Moreover, at this excitation wavelength, the quantum yield of NCSPS is 11.65 % (Fig.S5), significantly higher than the 5.21 % observed for NCQD films without SBA-15. This indirectly confirms that SBA-15 effectively reduces aggregation-induced quenching of NCQDs. Therefore, subsequent experiments will adopt 349 nm as the optimal excitation wavelength for NCSPS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of NCSPS to Cr (VI)
ce:section.ce:para: The initial discussion focused on the fluorescence response range of NCQDs@SBA15 to varying concentrations of Cr(VI). As illustrated in Fig. 5a, without the addition of Cr(VI), the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs@SBA15 peaks at an emission wavelength of 445 nm. Subsequently, as the concentration of Cr(VI) increased, the fluorescence intensity gradually diminished. By the time the concentration reached 1000 µM, the fluorescence quenching of NCQDs@SBA15 had surpassed 80 %.

To further ascertain the fluorescence response range of NCQDs@SBA15 and determine the detection limit for Cr(VI), a linear fitting was performed on the fluorescence intensity (F
0
/F) against the Cr(VI) concentration [40]. The results, as illustrated in Fig. 5b, indicate that F
0
/F exhibits a certain linear relationship with the concentration of Cr(VI). At lower Cr(VI) concentrations (0 ∼ 100 µM), the linear relationship is good and can effectively describe the relationship between the two, with the equation Y= 0.0054x+ 0.9728 (R
2 = 0.9941). The fluorescence response concentration range of NCQDs@SBA15 to Cr(VI) was set as 0 ∼ 100 µM. According to the calculation formula for the detection limit:
(2) LOD = 3σ/M
In the formula:
- M represents the slope of the linear relationship.
- σ represents the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity.
Through linear regression analysis of the fluorescence intensity as a function of Cr(VI) concentration, the detection limit of NCQDs@SBA15 for Cr(VI) was determined to be 43.7 nM.

In addition, to investigate whether the film interferes with the detection performance of NCQDs@SBA15, the response of NCSPS to different concentrations of Cr(VI) was further tested. As shown in Fig. 5c, similar to NCQDs@SBA15, NCSPS also exhibited excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior, the fluorescence intensity of NCSPS progressively decreased with increasing Cr(VI) concentrations (0–3000 μM). The linear fit of the fluorescence intensity data (F
0
/F) against the concentration of Cr(VI) is presented in Fig. 5d. The fluorescence response concentration range of NCSPS to Cr(VI) is between 0 ∼ 100 μM, with Y= 0.0037x+ 0.6313 (R
2 =0.9958), and a detection limit of 62.4 nM. Although fabricating it into a film slightly raised the detection limit of NCQDs@SBA15 for Cr(VI), it did not compromise its detection performance. Compared to other fluorescent probes that are not formulated into films (Table S2), NCSPS still has a lower detection limit for Cr(VI), which highlights its advantages as a fluorescent sensing platform for highly selective and sensitive detection of Cr(VI). At the same time, this greatly facilitates the use and storage of the fluorescent probe. This will contribute to the application of portable sensing probes in areas such as environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence Selectivity of NCSPS for Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: When evaluating the sensing performance of an inspection system in a practical environment, the excellent selectivity exhibited by the system towards the ions under test is crucial for accurately

assessing the quality of its sensing capabilities. This selectivity directly impacts the system's ability to identify and detect target ions, ultimately determining the accuracy and reliability of the sensor data. Experiments were conducted to investigate the selectivity of NCSPS towards metal ions. Eleven common metal ions were selected as interference ions, including Pb2+、Mg2+、K+、Na+、Mn2+、Ag+、Al3+、Cd2+、Ca2+、Zn2+and La3+. The fluorescence spectra of NCSPS were recorded after adding different metal ions. As illustrated in Fig. 6a, NCSPS exhibited the highest fluorescence quenching rate towards Cr(VI), while the impact of other metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of NCSPS was negligible. This demonstrates the excellent selectivity of NCSPS for Cr(VI) [41]. For specific details on the selectivity analysis of NCQDs@SBA15, please refer to Supporting Information Fig.S8.

To validate the interference resistance of NCSPS in Cr(VI) detection, similar quenching experiments were conducted by adding various metal ions. Even when other metal ions are present at equivalent concentrations, NCSPS consistently demonstrates excellent performance in detecting Cr(VI), as depicted in Fig. 6b. The experimental results indicated that the addition of interfering ions did not impede fluorescence quenching and providing a solid foundation and strong guarantee for the detection of Cr(VI) innatural water samples or complex wastewater [42].

The optical stability is one of the crucial optical properties that need to be considered for fluorescent probes [43]. The fluorescence intensity variation over time of NCQDs@SBA15 and NCSPS was investigated. NCQDs@SBA15 and NCSPS were kept in room temperature conditions without light shielding, and their fluorescence intensity was measured regularly every week for 7 weeks. As shown in Fig. 6(c-d), with the passage of time, both NCQDs@SBA15 and NCSPS exhibited no notable decrease in fluorescence intensity. After 7 weeks of natural light exposure, the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs@SBA15 decreased by approximately 12 %, while that of NCSPS only decreased by 7 %. This result suggests that NCQDs@SBA15 possesses good optical stability, and its binding with the NCSPS film is relatively stable, without separation over time. Moreover, the slight enhancement of optical stability in the NCSPS compared to NCQDs@SBA15 suggests that the formation of carbon quantum dots into a film structure minimizes the influence of time on its fluorescence intensity. This may be attributed to the reduced contact with air when NCQDs@SBA15 is fixed in the film, thereby avoiding some of the effects of air oxidation [44]. Overall, NCSPS exhibits excellent optical stability. The details of the thermal stability analysis of NCSPS can be found in the supporting information, Fig.S6.

Fig. 6(e-f) depicts digital images of PVA/SA film and NCSPS under UV lamp irradiation. It can be clearly observed that NCSPS emits strong and highly uniform blue fluorescence under 349 nm UV light excitation. In contrast, PVA/SA film does not exhibit any fluorescence under UV light irradiation. Additionally, detailed analyses of NCSPS and PVA/SA films under sunlight can be found in Section 2.2.2 of the revised Supporting Information. As shown in Fig.S9, the introduction of NCQDs@SBA-15 results in an uneven surface for NCSPS, whereas the PVA/SA film exhibits a smooth and flat surface. The uneven surface of NCSPS significantly increases the contact area with the test solution, facilitating the rapid penetration of Cr(VI) molecules into the NCSPS matrix, thereby ensuring detection efficiency [45]. Furthermore, we measured the water contact angles of both films (Fig.S7), and the results demonstrate that glutaraldehyde vapor cross-linking substantially enhanced the water resistance of NCSPS, ensuring its potential for practical applications.
To explore the fluorescence detection mechanism of NCSPS induced by Cr(VI), a series of experimental studies were conducted.

Among different fluorescence quenching mechanisms, static quenching typically requires the formation of a ground-state complex between the fluorophore and the quencher, accompanied by changes in the absorption spectrum [46]. However, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of NCSPS mixed with Cr(VI) showed no new absorption peaks (Fig. 6g), indicating no complex formation and ruling out the possibility of static quenching.

A comparison between the absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) and the excitation and emission spectra of NCSPS (Fig. 6h) revealed a significant overlap of Cr(VI)'s absorption spectrum with the excitation spectrum of NCSPS at 349 nm. This spectral overlap enables Cr(VI) to effectively quench the fluorescence of NCSPS, granting NCSPS specificity in recognizing Cr(VI). The quenching mechanism is thus proposed to be fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [47], a process that depends on spectral overlap to transfer energy from the donor (NCSPS) to the acceptor (Cr(VI)).

FRET typically requires the distance between the donor and acceptor to be less than 10 nm [48]. In aqueous solutions, Cr(VI) predominantly exists in the negatively charged form of HCrO4 - [49]. Surface charge measurements (Fig.S10) revealed that NCSPS carries a positive surface charge due to the presence of protonated amino groups (-NH3 +) [50]. The electrostatic interaction (EI) between the positively charged NCSPS surface and negatively charged HCrO4 - facilitates the adsorption of Cr(VI) onto the NCSPS surface, bringing the donor and acceptor into proximity and enabling FRET. The enhanced electrostatic interaction further supports the occurrence of the FRET mechanism.

Another key characteristic of FRET is the shortening of the donor's fluorescence lifetime [51]. Experimental results showed that the fluorescence lifetime of NCSPS was 8.77 ns in the absence of Cr(VI), which decreased to 7.89 ns after the addition of Cr(VI) (Fig. 6i). This reduction in fluorescence lifetime directly indicates the occurrence of FRET, as energy transfer leads to a decrease in the donor's fluorescence lifetime. However, factors such as the dominance of non-FRET donors in the overall decay signal and quenching rates exceeding the detection range can affect the results. Therefore, changes in fluorescence lifetime serve only as a reference and not as the primary evidence [52].

Using NCSPS to detect Cr(VI) in actual water samples, we observed significant color changes within a similar range of Cr(VI) concentrations as observed when testing deionized water. This indicates that the prepared NCSPS are suitable for visually detecting Cr(VI) in real water samples. Additionally, RGB analysis of fluorescence images of the actual samples showed quantification results for Cr(VI) similar to those obtained from deionized water. After calculation, we determined the recovery rate of Cr(VI) in the Songhua River water to be between 93.41 % and 97.06 %. This further confirms the reliable quantitative detection capability of the method for Cr(VI) in actual water samples.

To enhance the ease of detection, a portable, integrated concentration detection platform based on smartphones was developed. With the introduction of this system, images are captured, and the RGB values of the target area are identified to establish the relationship between RGB values and Cr(VI) concentration, thus achieving rapid detection [53]. The platform primarily consists of a smartphone fluorescence sensing device and an application for data processing. As depicted in Fig. 7b, the smartphone fluorescence sensing device is outfitted with an enclosed dark chamber, a sample platform, and UV light source. After capturing images using a smartphone, a self-developed application is used to read and identify the image information in order to obtain its corresponding RGB values. This process enables the application to infer the concentration of Cr(VI) in the water sample. Our research group has developed a "Cr(VI) Rapid Detection" image recognition application, as illustrated in Fig. 7c. The main menu of the "Cr(VI) Rapid Detection" application consists of two sections. The first section is used to obtain RGB values from images. Users select the image selection button to choose the image for detection, then select the button to obtain RGB values, and the results are clearly displayed on the interface. The obtained RGB values, combined with the formula (1) mentioned earlier, yield the actual RGB values. The second section is concentration analysis, primarily utilized for rapid on-site detection of Cr(VI) concentration. Users can ascertain the concentration of Cr(VI) in the water sample by inputting actual RGB values.

Simultaneously, to expand the practical application of the prepared NCSPS, we further divided the film into small strips, enabling portable on-site detection of actual environmental samples. To substantiate the benefits of portable Smartphone fluorescence sensing platform concerning detection limit and time, we conducted the following experiments. Fig. 7b displays digital photos of NCSPS under UV irradiation after the addition of different concentration gradients of Cr(VI). Upon 349 nm UV light irradiation, owing to the uniform distribution of NCQDs@SBA15, every hue across the entire test strip appears highly uniform. The smartphone captures images of NCSPS with varying concentrations of Cr(VI) added, enabling clear observation of evident color changes. As the concentration of Cr(VI) increases from 0 μM to 3000 μM, the test strip undergoes a color transition from bright blue to light blue, and finally to dark yellow. Subsequently, the RGB values of each image were identified using the "Cr(VI) Rapid Detection" application, and a correlation graph was established between the RGB values and the Cr(VI) concentrations. Fig. 7d depicts the relationship between the RGB values and the Cr(VI) concentrations. It is evident from the graph that there is a linear relationship between the RGB values and the Cr(VI) concentrations, with the RGB values inversely proportional to the Cr(VI) concentrations [54]. When conducting field tests for Cr(VI), the concentration in the water sample can be quickly determined using the second section of the main menu in our custom-made "Cr(VI) Rapid Detection" application. The smartphone fluorescence sensing platform has achieved a detection limit of 76 nM, with a linear range spanning from 0 to 100 μM, placing it in a more advantageous position compared to the detection limits of other smartphone sensing platforms (Table S3). Blind repeated measurements of the fluorescence response of NCSPS + Cr(VI) (20 μM) resulted in a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.25 % (n = 6), underscoring the outstanding repeatability of the measurement method [55].

Therefore, the study's outcomes indicate that the smartphone sensing platform developed herein provides an efficient and portable approach for on-site quantitative monitoring of Cr(VI). Compared to traditional liquid-phase detection systems or other instrument platforms, this system achieves the same objective but in a more convenient manner, while also offering unique comprehensive advantages concerning detection limit and detection time [56]. The specific details regarding the detection limit achieved in this work are summarized in
Table 2. This approach effectively mitigates errors stemming from environmental and operational factors and resolves issues related to image acquisition and data analysis. Portable devices leveraging high-performance NCSPS and smartphones demonstrate the capacity for on-site quantitative detection, boasting high sensitivity and low background noise. Consequently, they offer promising prospects for environmental sensing applications.

In this work, the fluorescent responsive film was successfully fabricated. Serving as an excitation sensing platform, NCSPS exhibits remarkable stability and resilience against interference. Notably, NCSPS exhibits high sensitivity in detecting Cr(VI), boasting a detection limit of 62.4 nM and a broad linear detection range. These findings suggest that NCSPS holds great promise as a detection platform for Cr(VI), positioning it as a highly favorable candidate for fluorescent detection materials. The occurrence of a strong electrostatic interaction between NCSPS and negatively charged Cr(VI) creates favorable conditions for the FRET mechanism, leading to fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, the NCSPS fluorescent responsive film can be conveniently fabricated into fluorescent test strips, particularly suitable for scenarios requiring rapid on-site metal ion content detection. By integrating these small strips with a smartphone-based handheld device, along with a self-developed application and RGB analysis, it becomes feasible to swiftly determine the concentration of Cr(VI) in water samples on-site, with a detection limit of 76 nM. This approach offers advantages such as speed, stability, environmental friendliness, portability, and cost-effectiveness. Overall, this platform provides a practical foundation for portable fluorescence detection.

Su Miao: Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jing Cai: Software, Resources, Investigation. Hu Jiabao: Writing – original draft, Software, Project administration, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yuan Haosen: Software, Resources, Project administration. Wang Binsong: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Li Bihao: Software, Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the NSFC project (21971057), with funding provided by the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (YQ2019B006) and Foundation of Heilongjiang Educational Committee (12531009).
